Automatic DQ? No, but it certainly warrants a higher level of due diligence (which you’re pursuing). At the very least, a low shoulder would affect the price to be paid, since the risk of an OTH bottle goes up substantially with that much ullage.

Bruce

posts.name: Andrew L.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Neal.Mollen:

… If a bottle looks otherwise sound, would a low shoulder fill stop you?

A Bordeaux with a low shoulder fill DOESN´T LOOK SOUND!
The odds that it will still deliver kind of positive drinking pleasure are very low …

I´ve had a lot of mid, upper-mid shoulder levels that were definitely affected, very few below midshoulder were drinkable …
Yes, a mid, even slightly below-mid shoulder level sometimes can be ok, but all low sh. bottles I had were “off”, too oxidized, not enjoyable …

The only case I woud buy such a bottle: it is A) a rare and very interesting wine from a good vintage,
and B) very cheap … than I would maybe take the odds 1:10 … or 1:20

PS: I´m talking about Bordeaux !

I find this a little surprising. I’ve had some great bottles from the '50s and '60s with levels like this, most spectacularly a 1959 Mouton back in 2010, but also humbler wines—Chasse Spleen '62, for example.

The rule I’ve heard it that if the ullage is uniform within a case / cellar, then there is no cause for concern. If, on the other hand, the bottle has a much more extreme ullage than other bottles in the case / cellar, then it is an indicator that something may be wrong. For bottles that have just been sitting quietly in the same place for the last five decades, I wouldn’t write it off—just take it as a sign to drink up. On the other hand, if the bottle has been traded a lot then maybe it’s more alarming.

posts.name: William Kelley

George Chadwick:

Personally, I wouldn’t touch a '66 with a low shoulder fill.

This. I don’t think 1966 except maybe someone said Cheval Blanc has the oomph to withstand that much oxygen. This is based mostly on a few bottles of 1966 Leoville Las Cases which needs vts to be even decent.

1966 is a strong Médoc vintage, but Las Cases were not making particularly interesting wines back then in my experience (limited to the '61, so not by any means conclusive). Recently a '66 Haut Batailley was really surprisingly good, probably the best I’ve ever had along with their 2010.

The vintage was high acid and high tannin so built to last; some of them are still a bit mean and lean, but the serious growths are certainly not going to go into precipitous decline any time soon.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: There’s just such a perceived high likelihood of a bad wine with low shoulder, at any vintage. Even if it was accurately provided how it came to be that way. Always a pass for me on anything with fill below shoulder.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Does it mean that any fill level below what the level of when the wine was bottled means that some type of seepage happened?

If so, then that means some type of bad seal, mishandling, or poor storage occurred during it’s lifetime. That would lead me to pass on said bottle with no exceptions. The assumption is that the wine is damaged, and no discount is worth the gamble to me. I’m sure it isn’t the ONLY bottle of that particular wine out there, and even if it was I’d buy a more recent vintage.

In 2011 I had a 1966 Lascombes that was still in the neck. I think there’s bound to be some variation given cork. However, I also think that it was my added care of allowing the wine to settle for 6 months at a 45 degree angle that allowed the wine to truly show it’s best.

Recently I have been drinking a fair amount of mid 1960s wine and a 1964 Tertre (Margaux) with a good level was quite nice (decent fill), a 66 Corton-Bressandes (Jaboulet-Vercherre) was really good and a 69 Clos de Tart (really low fill) turned out to be quite decent.

I’d worry more about signs of seepage than fill, though every old bottle is at best a bet but the good ones are quite rewarding. And of course I would try not to pay too much for low fills.

There is natural evaporation through the cork over time. Very slow, but it’s there. The cork is not a perfect seal. It lets “fresh” air in and it lets “humid” air out.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.cooked:
I have been reading this thread with great interest as I love old wines, but must purchase them as I have only been collecting for a few years.

A search of the internet show up not too much real data or research on the implications of age/evaporation/ullage on wine, in any way independent of just general aging.

When I consider this subject, the only thing there seems to be broad agreement about is that excess ullage for wine of a given age may be a sign of mishandling or bad storage, that is, an indicator rather than a problem in itself.

There are also indications that many feel that greater ullage in older wines is not just expected, but at least somewhat acceptable. This attitude suggests that the simple exchange of air and wine inevitable to long aging does not necessarily pose a problem.

So…if the exchange itself over time is OK when it takes place over many years of (apparently) good storage, what exactly is the problem with more ullage (within limits, I suppose) other than as an indicator of mishandling?

With 2016 approaching I am (for easily understandable reasons) on the lookout for 1966 bottles. I got a couple of Pommard so far which didn’t cost too much (around 50). I am now looking for some Bordeaux and it seems that Palmer was one of the best wines in that vintage. Prices, however, get up to 300.
I have now just seen a Margaux offered at auction for about half as that (start price), with fill level higher than what shown in this thread. I would say between mid- and high-shoulder.
Questions for the experts:

  • would that fill level (M-HS) be ok?
  • I imagine the Palmer’s price reflects a higher fill level (I just asked for a picture). If this is so, would you suggest going for the Palmer?
  • for bottles in similar conditions (and similar price) would the Palmer still be a better bet?

Thanks in advance.

posts.name: John Danza
posts.cooked:
Neal, did the retailer give you any info on the storage conditions this bottle had seen in it’s life, to their knowledge?

As others have said, this really matters as much as anything because the ullage can happen over time. I’ve picked bottles out of an old cellar where a case was stored in the bin from the beginning and the bottles had all different levels of ullage. I paid a fair price for those bottles that were still high-shoulder/base neck fill, but paid a good percentage less for those that were as low as mid-shoulder. They all still drunk well.

I stay away from anything lower than mid-shoulder, no matter how old it is. There’s just too much risk unless they practically give the bottle away.

BTW, my vote would be to slow-ox the bottle instead of decanting. I find it works better for wines of this age. My $0.02.

I look forward to your report on how it turned out. Best of luck.
